title: Name a Feeling
summary: Identify and name the emotion you are experiencing right now.
learnerPromise: You will be able to put what you are feeling into a simple, accurate word.
whyItMatters: Naming a feeling helps you understand yourself better and makes it easier to decide what you need. It is often the first step toward handling a difficult moment well.
realLifeUses: You feel uneasy before meeting new people and realize the word is 'nervous'
You are short-tempered after a long day and name it as 'frustrated'
You feel light and content after a good conversation and call it 'happy' or 'grateful'
You feel torn between two choices and recognize the feeling as 'conflicted'
result: A named emotion from a recent personal experience.
youWillLearn: How to tell the difference between similar feelings like angry, frustrated, and disappointed
How to check in with yourself and find the right word
Why putting a name to a feeling can make it feel more manageable
How this skill helps when talking to others about what you need
miniChallenge: Think about the last 24 hours. What is one moment where you felt something strongly? Write down the situation and one word that describes what you felt.
steps: Take a moment to pause and think about how you are feeling right now
Ask yourself: Is this feeling pleasant, unpleasant, or mixed?
Think about what caused the feeling — a person, a thought, or an event
Pick one word from the emotion list or from your own vocabulary that fits best
Write the feeling down and say it quietly to yourself
completionCriteria: You identified and wrote down one named emotion from a real experience
You can explain what made you feel that way
commonProblems: You might draw a blank and not know what word fits — this is common and gets easier
You may judge the feeling as good or bad — try to notice it without judging
Some feelings are mixed or complicated — one word may not cover everything, and that is okay
tips: Keep a short list of emotion words handy: happy, sad, angry, nervous, frustrated, relieved, grateful, lonely, excited, tired, conflicted
It is okay to feel more than one thing at once — name the strongest one first
The more you practice, the faster you will be able to name feelings in the moment
